test_text.title: Market Research for a New Television Channel

audioscript.text: Section 1, you will hear someone being asked their opinion of a new television station.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Excuse me, I'm sorry to bother you, but would you have time to answer a few questions?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, what's it about?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We're doing some market research for a new television channel starting in 2 years' time.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. OK, why not?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Lovely, we'll just work through this form.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And if we could start with some personal background information?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: If I could just have your age?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Great.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The respondent is 35 years old, so b has been circled.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully to the conversation and answer questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Excuse me, I'm sorry to bother you, but would you have time to answer a few questions?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, what's it about?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We're doing some market research for a new television channel starting in 2 years' time.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. OK, why not?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Lovely, we'll just work through this form.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And if we could start with some personal background information?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Right, if I could just have your age?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right. Great.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And your job?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. Systems analyst, uh, but for the form, uh, I don't know whether it would count as professional or business or what.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: What do you think?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. OK, it's more like business.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hmm, fine.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And would you mind my asking about your salary?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Or we can leave it blank.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: No, no, I don't mind.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's £40,000 a year.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, thank you.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right, about your current watching habits.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: What would you say is your main reason for watching TV?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, at work, I tend to read for information, and what have you, so, I'd say that with Tv, it probably just helps me relax and unwind.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Fine, and how many hours a day on average do you watch TV?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hmm. Not a lot really, I should say just over an hour.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: You now have some time to look at questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So what are the two main times of the day that you watch TV?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, uh, a little around breakfast time, and then it tends to be really late, um, 11 or even midnight when I finish work.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And what sort of programs do you go for?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Some news bulletins, but I also really like to put my feet up with some of the old comedy shows.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And turning to the new channel.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Which type of programs would you like to see more of?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, I certainly don't think we need any more factual programs like news and documentaries.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I think we need more about things like local information, you know, providing a service for the community.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And in the same vein, perhaps more for younger viewers, you know, good quality stuff.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Ah ha. And if you had to give the new directors some specific advice when they set up the channel what advice would you give them?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I think I'd advise them to pay a lot of attention to the quality of the actual broadcast, you know, the sound system.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: People are very fussy these days about that.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And in general, I think they ought to do lots more of these kinds of interview, you know, talking with their potential customers.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, I'm glad you think it's valuable.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Certainly, yeah.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: This will be a commercial channel of course, but how often do you think it is tolerable to have adverts?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, out of that list, I'd say every quarter of an hour.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I don't think we can complain about that, as long as they don't last for 10 minutes each time.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Quite.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And would you be willing to attend any of our special promotions for the new channel?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, yeah, I'd be very happy to, as long as they're held here in my area.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: OK, I'll make a note of that.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And finally, may we put you on our mailing list?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, um, I prefer not, except for the information about the promotion you mentioned.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Can I have your name and address?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Of course.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Here's my card.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, lovely.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And thank you very much for your time, and we look forward to seeing you.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, indeed.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Um. Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Social Facilities on Campus and in Town

audioscript.text: Section 2, you will hear a student welfare officer talking to a small group of students, just before the beginning of term.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully to the talk, and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Uh. OK, uh, well, good morning everybody.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: My name is Elizabeth Reed, and I'm your assistant welfare officer.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: Uh. What I'd like to do now is tell you a little more about some of the social facilities available on the campus, and also to tell you something about what the town has to offer.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: As you probably know already, the Student Union building is the main center of social life here, as indeed it is in most British universities.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: The union runs a weekly program of events for all tastes.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: Oh, everything from discos to talks by guest speakers.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: Many of these events are fundraising activities for charities, which the union takes very seriously.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: They manage the students' union paper shop, selling magazines and newspapers, as well as stationery, sweets, and so on.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: Um. Then uh, let me see, oh, there's the ticket shop, where you can get some very good deals on, well, for example, coaches to London, or inexpensive charter flights.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: As cheap as you'll get anywhere, people say, or tickets for big pop groups playing here, or at other venues all over the country, or plays in London.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: Oh.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: And we mustn't forget the Union Cafeteria and the big new diner.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: Er... yes? Did you have a question?
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: Uh. Yeah, does the union also provide help with any problems?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: I mean advice on financial problems, for example.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Or does the university provide that?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Yes, the union runs their own advice service, offering help with financial matters, uh, such as grants.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: I'm sure you realize anything medical should be discussed with the University Medical Service, which also has an excellent counseling center.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: I think that was made clear yesterday.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: However, the union has its own officer who can give advice on legal problems.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: You now have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen to the rest of the talk, and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now on to Radford.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: For a town of its size, Radford has some unusually good leisure and community facilities, and has quite a good shopping center with an interesting range of shops.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: As you go into Radford, there's a new, well, quite new, Olympic size swimming pool.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: Uh. That's on the outskirts at a place called Renton.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: Above the pool, there's a high tech fitness center.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: Are there any ice skaters here?
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: No, oh pity, uh, the facilities for ice skating are excellent.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: Well, uh, the new Metro Tower right in the center of town has got an ice rink.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: And a sports hall for squash, badminton, volleyball, and several other indoor sports.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: And in the same building there's a new cinema with six screens.
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: Uh. Then, let me see, um, in the main square, just two minutes' walk from the Metro Tower, there's the Theater Royal, which often gets London productions on tour...
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: and in the streets nearby, you can find a good range of inexpensive restaurants, including Indian, Chinese, Thai, and...
audioscript.speaker: ELIZABETH

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which FOUR activities of the Union are mentioned by the speaker?
Which TWO of the following can you get advice about from the Union?

list: part of the Metro Tower building
in the main square in the centre of the town
some distance from the centre of the town
body.listTitle: What are the locations of the following places in Radford?

audioscript.text: Section 3, you will hear a tutor, Doctor Simon, talking to some of his students about writing a dissertation.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen to the discussion and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Uh, OK, yeah.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Welcome back to the new term.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: I hope you've had a good break, and that you're looking forward to writing your dissertation.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Now, what I'd like to do in this session is give you the opportunity to ask questions on writing the dissertation.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Uh. Requirements, milestones, who to see when you need help.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: It's very informal.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: It may all be written on paper, but it's nice to get it confirmed.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: So, um, anything you'd like to ask?
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Uh. Doctor Simon.
audioscript.speaker: ANDY

audioscript.text: Is there a fixed hand in date yet?
audioscript.speaker: ANDY

audioscript.text: Uh. Right, I can confirm that that's the 21st of May, not the 20th as we first stated.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: OK?
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Um. Jane?
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: What about the word limit?
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Well we try to be pretty flexible on this, but in broad terms it's 18 to 20,000.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Ah...
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: And you can choose your topics, anything from years two and three.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Uh. Yes?
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Uh. I still haven't got any idea what I want to do it on.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Uh, uh. Who?
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Well, you should see your course tutor to agree on your final title, and you should also be aware that there's a special program running on research methods for anyone who wants some extra help on that.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Can I just check on the deadlines for everything?
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Yes, sure.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Um. Look, uh, let me write it on the board.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Uh. When the different stages have to be completed.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: First of all, you've got to work on your basic bibliography.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: And that's due into your course tutor by 31st January, which is just two weeks away, so you'd better get a move on on that.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Do we have to have our own draft plan by then?
audioscript.speaker: ANDY

audioscript.text: No, your draft plan is due on the seventh of February.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Which is a week later, so that should give you plenty of time.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Uh. And when do we have to be doing the research?
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: That's over a one month period, essentially February to March.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: And the write up?
audioscript.speaker: ANDY

audioscript.text: Well, you can't really get going on your writing until you've got quite a bit of the research done, so that's really March to May, with the hand in date on the 21st. Any more questions?
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Now you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen to the rest of the discussion, and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Well well, sir, just some advice really.
audioscript.speaker: ANDY

audioscript.text: It's about computers.
audioscript.speaker: ANDY

audioscript.text: Would you advise us to buy one?
audioscript.speaker: ANDY

audioscript.text: Uh. What can I say?
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Um. Andy, I know it's a massive expense, but I really feel it would be of great benefit.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: You could always look in the student union adverts for second-hand ones.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Um. Yes, I've been looking at some of last year's dissertations.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Oh, is that a good idea, sir?
audioscript.speaker: ANDY

audioscript.text: I heard.
audioscript.speaker: ANDY

audioscript.text: Well, I don't think you should read them in detail too early.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Or you might end up taking more of their ideas than you realize.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Um. But yes, it really is the best guide you can have to the expectations of the of what's expected when you write a dissertation.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Sorry, jane.
audioscript.speaker: ANDY

audioscript.text: I interrupted you.
audioscript.speaker: ANDY

audioscript.text: That's OK.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: It's just that they did a lot of research using questionnaires.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Is that a good idea?
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: I think questionnaires are very good at telling you how people who fill out questionnaires feel, uh, but to be frank, they tell you very little else.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Avoid them.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: About interviews, is it OK if we interview you?
audioscript.speaker: ANDY

audioscript.text: The tutors?
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: I don't see why not.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: They don't have any special contribution to make, but you can if you want.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: There's a whole section on this issue in the research guide.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: I'm afraid it's slightly out of date, and you're probably better talking to the tutor on the research methods course, but you might find it useful to start there.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: OK, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: ANDY

audioscript.text: OK, well, great.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Uh. I hope that sorted a few things out.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: You can always come and see me, or drop me a note if you got any more queries.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: OK, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: DR SIMON

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.listTitle: What is Dr Simon's opinion on the following points?

answer_key.correct_answer: do research | do the research | research | carry out research | conduct research

audioscript.text: Section 4, you will hear a lecture about the lack of rainfall in Australia.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen to the lecture and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, uh, this morning, we are continuing our look at Australia and its natural problems.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Actually dryness or aridity as it is generally called by geographers, is probably the most challenging of Australia's natural problems.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: And so it's very important in this course for you to have a good understanding of the subject.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: For Australia, water is a precious resource, and its wise management is of the greatest importance.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: As I've said, Australia is a dry continent, second only to Antarctica in its lack of rainfall. Long hours of hot sunshine and searing winds give Australia an extremely high rate of evaporation, far more than in most other countries.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: It's estimated that approximately 87% of Australia's rainfall is lost through evaporation.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Compared with just over 60% in Europe and Africa, and 48% in North America.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: You generally think of Africa as being a very hot and dry place.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: But it's not, in comparison with Australia.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: In many parts of Australia, standing water, that is dams, puddles, and so forth.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Dry up rapidly, and some rainfall barely penetrates the soil.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: The reason for this is that the moisture is absorbed by thirsty plants.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Some parts of Australia are dry, because rainwater seeps quickly through sandy soils and into the rock below.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: In parts of Australia, this water which seeps through the sandy soil collects underground to form underground lakes.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Water from these subterranean lakes can be pumped to the surface and tapped.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: And so used for various purposes above the ground.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: In fact, extensive underground water resources are available over more than half of Australia's land area.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: But most of the water is too salty to be used for human consumption, or for the irrigation of crops.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: However, most inland farmers do rely on this water for watering their animals.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: And where possible, to a lesser extent for irrigation.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Underground water can flow very large distances, and can be kept in underground reservoirs for a very long time.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Water from these underground reservoirs bubbles to the surface as springs in some parts of the country, and these rare sources of permanent water were vital to early explorers of inland Australia.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: And to other pioneers last century who used the springs for survival.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: But in many places levels have fallen drastically through continuous use over the years.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: This has necessitated the pumping of the water to the surface.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Remarkably, underground water sources in Australia supply about 18% of total water consumption.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: So you can see it's quite an important source of water in this dry land.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: So, most of the consumption of water in Australia comes from water which is kept above ground.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: More than 300 dams regulate river flows around the country.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: The dams store water for a variety of functions, the rural irrigation of crops, without which many productive areas of the country would not be able to be farmed.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: The regulation of flooding, a serious problem which will be dealt with later in the course, and last but not least, the harnessing of the force of gravity.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: For the generation of electricity.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Uh. That is all we have time for this morning, but you'll be able to do further study on this important area in the library.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: I have a handout here with references on the subject, so if you're interested please come up to the desk and take a copy.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: Next week's lecture is a Case Study of an outback farm, and it'll be going into detail about some of the problems we've discussed here.
audioscript.speaker: LECTURER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: The evaporation rate in Australia is
Rainfall in Australia hardly penetrates the soil because
Water is mainly pumped up for
Natural springs are located
options: in a few areas of Australia.

body.title: Which THREE of the following uses of dam water are mentioned?
